title: Chapter 15
text: Chapter Twelve L ara lay in bed, staring through the window at the night sky. She’d opened the curtains and found the strings that lifted the plastic slats, allowing the orangey glow from the streetlights outside to stream in, and turned off the bedroom light. It was the same sky she’d seen for her whole life, but from here, it was unfamiliar . She wasn’t sure how long she lay like that; at some point, her eyes drifted shut . A sound pulled her from sleep. Mind clouded, she lifted her head and blinked the blurriness from her eyes. The door opened slowly. The hallway beyond was dark, but the light from the window settled on Ronin, whose broad-shouldered frame filled the doorway . Pushing herself up onto her elbows, Lara frowned. “ Ronin ?” He wore only pants, which hung loosely at his hips. His body was perfection, his sculpted muscles accentuated by the soft light and deep shadows. Unbidden, her eyes swept over him, drinking in his squared jaw, wide chest, chiseled abs, and the lines tapering from his hips to his pelvis . She swallowed hard, and her body responded, tightening with a deep ache in her belly. She squeezed handfuls of the blanket . Ronin didn’t move . “Is something wrong?” She forced her eyes to meet his . “No,” he finally said, and stepped toward her. He didn’t stop until he stood beside the bed, gazing down at her, eyes impossibly green despite the darkness . He pulled the blanket off Lara. The shirt she had worn to bed had ridden up to her hips, leaving her exposed. She reached down to cover herself, but Ronin took hold of her wrists before she could. The bare metal of his hands was warm . “What are you doing?” she asked, eyes wide as he climbed onto the bed and loomed over her, settling between her thighs. The bedding groaned under his weight . He guided her back down by her wrists, pinning them over her head. Cool air whispered over her sex, contrasting the heat of her body. Moisture gathered at her core . Ronin slipped a hand under her shirt and pressed his palm to her stomach. It was solid and smooth. Slowly, he slid it up. Her skin quivered beneath his touch. When his hand cupped her breast, she arched into it, nipple beading against his palm . “Ronin,” she rasped, breath shallow . Though his expression was blank, his intense eyes dipped to her mouth. He released her wrists and dipped down, chest pressing to hers, and their mouths connected . Lara wrapped her arms around him, closing her eyes. His lips were soft, molding to hers, but there was firmness beneath. They moved away, trailing over her jaw, her chin, her neck. One of his hands slid between her thighs, caressing her sex. She moaned . He leaned back, placed a hand on each of her knees, and spread her legs wide. She felt his cockhead against her entrance. Staring into her eyes, he pulled back and thrust into her . She awoke with a gasp and sat up. The gentle light from the street poured in through the window slats. The door was closed, and the blanket was bunched at the end of the bed. Her bare legs were spread, knees up, cold air on her exposed sex. It was wet enough to dampen the sheets . “Oh my God.” She squeezed her thighs together against the ache. Her breasts were heavy, nipples sensitive as they brushed against the fabric of her shirt. She swept hair out of her face . “No. Oh no, no, no.” Lara leapt out of the bed, tugging the hem of the shirt down. She grasped fistfuls of hair, barely feeling the sharp pain on her scalp as she paced in the darkness. “This can’t be happening. It can’t !” How was this possible after what she’d been through? Ronin was attractive, without a doubt, but he was a bot ! Hadn’t she learned her lesson about them ? It was because she’d danced for him. When he’d stopped her the first time and said he didn’t want it that way, she took it as a challenge. She wanted to get a rise out of him. To turn him on, if it was even possible. To torment him within the boundaries of their agreement, because he wasn’t allowed to touch her . She hadn’t expected that dancing for him would excite her . All the while, he’d worn a blank expression, and hadn’t moved an inch. He’d prove as immovable as a mountain. But his stare was heavy, and she’d been aware of it throughout . In the end, he hadn’t even enjoyed it. Her reaction, naturally, had been anger . So why had she dreamt of him? Why was she so aroused, when she should reject the very idea of his touch ? Because he was different . “Dammit, no!” She dropped onto the edge of the bed, pressing her fists into her thighs, and groaned . “ No .” She needed to forget about this. There was no telling how long he’d maintain interest in her, but she would have to dance for him if she wanted food and shelter. If she wanted him to help find Tabitha. But the only way to turn her thoughts away from Ronin was to dwell on the darkness of her past…and she refused to do that, even after she’d been betrayed by her body and her dreams . Swinging her legs onto the bed, she grabbed the wadded blanket and shook it over herself as she lay back down, tucking it under her backside so she didn’t feel the wet spot on the sheets. She didn’t need a reminder of her weakness . Lara closed her eyes. Time passed, and sleep refused to come . Creaking footsteps overhead jarred her. She stared up at the shadowed ceiling and listened to him move around, surprisingly quiet for how heavy he was. Why was he back up there? Was he as hungry for answers as Lara was? It was obvious that he’d never gone up into that room; everything within had been undisturbed, coated with years-worth of dust . She was tempted to pull on some pants and go up there with him, but the phantom of her dream swept over her mind. She couldn’t be around him right now. Not while she was uncertain of how she’d react . Not while the feel of him inside her was fresh in her memory . * * * R onin stood with his hands clasped behind his back, staring at the journal atop the chest. It lay amongst his tools, standing out from them despite being just as old and worn. The electrodes in his fingertips fired off, sending a twitch through his fingers . Just a glitch . He’d been too long in the Dust on his last run, had endured more wear and tear than normal. That was the reason for the minor, fleeting malfunction in his hand. It was not because his desire to pick up the book and read was warring with his concern for what he’d discover inside . The journal couldn’t do any harm. It was just ink on paper. All the words in the world, all the thoughts, could accomplish nothing without action. And whatever actions were described on those pages had concluded many years ago . He stepped up to the chest and reached down, lifting the book from its place. Paper and ink. Its weight was barely enough to register to him, as airy and meaningless as the information it held . If only he believed that . Holding the journal on his palm, he opened it to the first page . Those things killed people today. Marched into town and just started killing people. I watched from the window as people were dragged into the park and executed . The last time Ronin had looked over the park, it was peaceful, bathed in evening sunlight. Trees and grass swayed in the breeze, and glittering ripples skittered across the surface of the pond . Though he could have read the page entirely in the time it took to glance at it, he forced himself to slow down, to analyze each word . It doesn’t look like they’re going house-to-house yet, which is probably the only reason I’m still here to write this. Pretty sure the Joneses and the Ortegas were down there. I didn’t notice anyone else that looked familiar, but I wasn’t about to press my face to the glass and show them where I’m hiding . Night’s already falling, but everything is bright across the street. The fire’s still burning. I’m glad the wind is moving west, or I’d have to smell them . The page ended there, with the bottom half blank. Ronin turned to the next . Found out from Mandy Weiss earlier this morning that the shit show in the park was because THEY wanted part of the city cleared out. Said it was THEIR right to inhabit that part of Cheyenne, as it’s their kind that’s kept it running. People here have been scared for years – since well before the damned world ended – and there’s nothing beyond the city but dust and radiation. Where the hell were those people supposed to go ? She told me the leader of the robots gave the humans the day to pack up and leave their homes. Most stayed. When 24 hours passed – ex-fucking-zactly 24 hours, down to the second – those tin bastards started going into the buildings and dragging people out . It’s only a matter of time before they start clearing out more of these homes. I’ll have to keep my ear to the ground . Ronin’s brow furrowed. He’d been to places where humans and bots lived apart from one another, but the separation usually seemed a matter of comfort and practicality. Flesh and bone had different needs than metal and electronics. He couldn’t recall hearing of such open, widescale hostility between the two before . At least the entry gave him a probable time frame. People here have been scared for years — since well before the damned world ended . That had to be referring to the Black Out . Realized I haven’t been dating this. Guess it doesn’t really matter, though. Probably won’t be anyone left to read this in a decade or two, at the rate we’re going. God, we made mistakes, but did we really deserve all this ? Been three days since they burned the bodies. It’s mostly been quiet, though Mandy told me there’ve been a few brawls amongst the other people left in town. Lots of tension in the air. I went down to the bar on 19 th to see for myself. They still got booze. A week ago I would’ve said that was the only reason a place like that could be so busy, but I think that’s wrong now. I got the sense that there were so many folks there because everyone’s scared and nobody wants to be alone right now . This town took enough of a hit when the armies rolled through the first time. We weathered the worst of it. Hell, who the fuck would bother targeting Wyoming, apart from the oil? We’ve maybe had it too easy. Sounds like it’s gone to shit everywhere else. Every now and then someone drifts up from Colorado or wanders in along I-80 from further west, and none of them have good stories to tell. They say Denver’s just a radioactive crater . Now we’ve got these robot sons of bitches coming in and telling us what parts of town we can’t go into anymore. How the hell’s that acceptable? I bet some of them were even part of the group that came through during the war. For the most part, they left the city alone. Most of the fighting was at the old Air Force Base – we still see smoke from there, sometimes, on the clear days – but there was still damage done . They were talking down at the bar like we’re all going to get together and do something about this. Gather weapons and force them out. Sure, there are bots that aren’t causing any trouble, but nobody seems to care about them now. They’re all a problem, it was them that fought in the war, them that was killing women and children . All those idiots already forgot it was us that started dropping the bombs . I’m getting myself all worked up. Got to stop. I’m likely to do something stupid when I get this way . Ronin stopped, placed his finger between the pages, and closed the book around it. Was there any analysis he could perform on what he’d just read that wouldn’t come away confused ? Humans skewed their perception of events based on their emotions. He knew that firsthand, thanks largely to recent experiences. The journal’s writer even alluded to it in his entry, perhaps realizing it after he looked up to see the steady degradation of his handwriting, the increasing pressure with each pen- stroke . Ronin entered the hallway and walked to the attic hatch. A first glance rarely revealed everything. Reaching up, he grasped the string and opened the hatch. The ladder protested his ascent, and the floorboards creaked despite his slow, careful steps. Standing by the window, he was directly above Lara’s room . What would she say when he told her what the journal contained? I knew it , or doesn’t surprise me , or perhaps you fucking buckets-of-bolts bastards ? Or, would her lips turn down and her eyes glisten with sorrow he couldn’t comprehend, sorrow for the memory of long-dead people she’d never known? For people who were taken from their homes — from this house — and killed ? He stopped at the window and stared out, taking in the scene. The park was mostly dark, save for the light reflected on the pond; it was a patch of black between the gentle glow of streetlamps on either side. A view that must have been familiar to the man who wrote the journal . Ronin ran his optics over the blankets and magazines on the floor, the jars of food in the bookcase, the table with its single chair. What would it have been like to be in the place of the writer ? He reached through his memory, accessing data from his many forays into, around, and beyond the Dust. Almost every night had been spent alone, and he’d never given that fact any thought. He’d encountered plenty of people out there, leaving most dead or deactivated in the dirt . The Dust favored the quick and the ruthless. There was no camaraderie to be found out there . Yet hadn’t he encountered, time and again, bots and humans alike who ran in groups? A pair of synths, half a dozen humans, all eager for supplies, for food, for the imminent, violent confrontation with Ronin that was likely their only social interaction outside their small group ? Somehow, that thought chain brought him to a new realization — it was possible that he didn’t have to go out alone. Lara was a survivor. Like any human, she had her weaknesses, but there was strength in her beyond his ability to classify and measure. What would it be like to walk the Dust with her? To converse with her over the long days and nights, to have an extra pair of eyes watching his back ? To have someone to watch out for other than himself . No. The Dust was no place for her. No place for anyone . The dangers to him were too great as it was, and he couldn’t consider putting her at such risk for even a moment . He opened the journal again, and was still reading when the rising sun crested the roof enough to cast its pure light on the treetops across the street . They’re coming for me , the last page began, the letters distorted by haste. I think everyone else is already gone, and in a few more days, they’ll have that wall finished and I’ll be trapped. I don’t know why I stayed this long. Probably because I can still remember the smiles on the faces of my wife and kids while we played at Holliday Park…maybe, if I make it out, I’ll see them out there, where the dust always blows . This is it, now. If you’re human, you’d better be the fuck outside this wall. During the big fight outside the bar on 19 th , someone cut the leader bot’s face, and now there’s no mercy, no warnings. This part of Cheyenne belongs to those things. We can have the rubble beyond . After all the damage and destruction we’ve faced, people haven’t learned a goddamned thing. They didn’t fight the bots for survival, they fought because they thought this place, these buildings, really matter. The lives we lived before…they’re gone, and we’re never getting them back. They’re The tail of that final letter trailed across the page like it had been slashed by a knife. Ronin stared at it while the sun came up, as trapped in place as the writer had been .
